coredata.dc:title: Smartphone-assisted paper-based analytical device integrated with photo-responsive oxidase mimetic for colorimetric detection of carbosulfan
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Haodong
Xia, Mingyuan
Yang, Wanqi
Liu, Hanmeng
Ni, Pengjuan
Lu, Yizhong
coredata.dc:description: Given the significant harm caused by carbosulfan, the prominent characteristics of photo-responsive nanozymes and the bright prospects of paper-based analytical devices (PADs), developing a photo-responsive nanozyme-mediated colorimetric PAD for the convenient and accurate detection of carbosulfan is highly desired. Herein, carbon dots/graphitic carbon nitride (C-dots/g-C3N4) photo-responsive oxidase mimetic is constructed and employed in the field of colorimetric PAD for carbosulfan detection. C-dots/g-C3N4 with excellent photo-responsive oxidase-like activity is obtained by controlling the loading amounts of C-dots on g-C3N4, which can be ascribed to the enhanced visible light-harvesting ability and more efficient charge separation and transfer. C-dots/g-C3N4 is capable of catalyzing the oxidation of 3,3’,5,5’-tetramethylbenzidine with noticeable color changes due to the formation of singlet oxygen and photogenerated holes. Whereas, the oxidase-like activity of C-dots/g-C3N4 is inhibited by the reductive sulfide compound derived from the hydrolysis of carbosulfan under acidic medium, thereby inhibiting the chromogenic reaction. Based on the above facts, a colorimetric PAD for carbosulfan detection is proposed. The designed PAD shows a good linear range of 5–60 μM with a detection limit down to 2.52 μM. Moreover, the PAD demonstrates high accuracy when detecting carbosulfan in real samples. This work may expand the applications of photo-responsive nanozymes in PADs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbosulfan
Photo-responsive nanozymes
Oxidase-like activity
Paper-based analytical devices
Carbon nitride

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of g-C3N4 and x% C-dots/g-C3N4
The photo-responsive oxidase-like activity of C-dots/g-C3N4
Impacts of different scavengers on the photo-responsive oxidase-like activity of C-dots/g-C3N4
Construction of the PAD for carbosulfan detection
Photo-responsive oxidase-like activity of g-C3N4 and C-dots/g-C3N4
Mechanism of the photo-responsive oxidase-like activity of C-dots/g-C3N4

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32202145 and 22172063), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2022QB192), the Independent Cultivation Program of Innovation Team of Jinan City (2021GXRC052), the Key Project of Anhui Provincial Department of Education (2023AH050869).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic diagram of the synthesis of g-C3N4 and C-dots/g-C3N4; TEM images of g-C3N4 (B) and 0.2 % C-dots/g-C3N4 (C-E); XRD patterns (F) and FT-IR spectra (G) of the as-prepared materials.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of different solutions, including g-C3N4 and 0.2 % C-dots/g-C3N4 in the absence and presence of light, and TMB under light irradiation; (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of the oxidation of TMB catalyzed by different materials; (C) Photo-controllable oxidase-like activity of 0.2 % C-dots/g-C3N4; Steady-state kinetic analysis (D), the corresponding Lineweaver-Burk curve (E) and SA (F) of 0.2 % C-dots/g-C3N4; Steady-state kinetic analysis (G), the corresponding Lineweaver-Burk curve (H) and SA (I) of g-C3N4.

UV–vis DRS (A), band structure diagram (B) of as-prepared materials (1-g-C3N4; 2–0.05 % C-dots/g-C3N4; 3–0.2 % C-dots/g-C3N4; 4–0.5 % C-dots/g-C3N4); PL emission spectra (C), EIS plots (D) and the photocurrent response curves (E) of g-C3N4 and 0.2 % C-dots/g-C3N4; (F) The UV-vis absorption spectra of 0.2 % C-dots/g-C3N4-TMB system at 652 nm under various atmospheres.

(A) Effects of different scavengers on TMB oxidation; EPR analysis of 0.2 % C-dots/g-C3N4 with TEMP aqueous solution (B), TEMPO aqueous solution (C), DMPO aqueous solution (D) and DMPO methanol solution (E); (F) Schematic diagram of the catalytic mechanism of C-dots/g-C3N4.

(A) Schematic diagram for detecting carbosulfan; (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of this sensing platform in the absence or presence of various concentrations of carbosulfan; (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of this sensing platform after the introduction of different concentrations of carbosulfan; (D) The relationship between ΔA and carbosulfan concentration, inset shows the corresponding linear relationship and color changes; (E) The selectivity and anti-interference ability of the detection system.

(A) Schematic illustration of carbosulfan detection based on the PAD assay; (B) The relationship between R/B value and carbosulfan concentrations; (C) The fitted linear relationship between R/B values and carbosulfan concentrations.
Schematic illustration of C-dots/g-C3N4 photo-responsive oxidase mimetic-mediated colorimetric PAD for carbosulfan detection.
The results of colorimetric detection carbosulfan in real samples.

head.ce:title: Smartphone-assisted paper-based analytical device integrated with photo-responsive oxidase mimetic for colorimetric detection of carbosulfan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Anhui University of Chinese Medicine, Hefei, Anhui 230012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Pharmacy, Anhui University of Chinese Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Anhui University of Chinese Medicine, Hefei 230012, Anhui, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Given the significant harm caused by carbosulfan, the prominent characteristics of photo-responsive nanozymes and the bright prospects of paper-based analytical devices (PADs), developing a photo-responsive nanozyme-mediated colorimetric PAD for the convenient and accurate detection of carbosulfan is highly desired. Herein, carbon dots/graphitic carbon nitride (C-dots/g-C3N4) photo-responsive oxidase mimetic is constructed and employed in the field of colorimetric PAD for carbosulfan detection. C-dots/g-C3N4 with excellent photo-responsive oxidase-like activity is obtained by controlling the loading amounts of C-dots on g-C3N4, which can be ascribed to the enhanced visible light-harvesting ability and more efficient charge separation and transfer. C-dots/g-C3N4 is capable of catalyzing the oxidation of 3,3’,5,5’-tetramethylbenzidine with noticeable color changes due to the formation of singlet oxygen and photogenerated holes. Whereas, the oxidase-like activity of C-dots/g-C3N4 is inhibited by the reductive sulfide compound derived from the hydrolysis of carbosulfan under acidic medium, thereby inhibiting the chromogenic reaction. Based on the above facts, a colorimetric PAD for carbosulfan detection is proposed. The designed PAD shows a good linear range of 5–60 μM with a detection limit down to 2.52 μM. Moreover, the PAD demonstrates high accuracy when detecting carbosulfan in real samples. This work may expand the applications of photo-responsive nanozymes in PADs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The pervasive utilization of pesticides in agriculture has made significant advancements in crop yield and protection against pests. However, the indiscriminate applications of pesticides has raised concerns about their influence on the environment and human health [1]. Carbosulfan, a potent carbamate pesticide, is highly effective against numerous insects. Despite its benefits, carbosulfan is extremely toxic to non-target organisms, including beneficial insects and aquatic life, and has been associated with many kinds of health issues in humans [2,3]. Moreover, carbosulfan is easily decomposed into carbofuran, which is also highly toxic to humans [4]. Therefore, detection of carbosulfan residues is crucial for protecting human health [5].

To date, chromatographic strategies have been employed to detect carbosulfan. Even though they show high sensitivity and good reproducibility, they are limited by the expensive equipment, complex and time-consuming operation steps [6,7]. Recently, paper-based analytical devices (PADs) have garnered significant attentions in terms of their low cost, ease of production, portability and ability to perform fast on-site analysis. A variety of analytical techniques, such as fluorescence [8–10], colorimetry [11], electrochemistry [12] and chemiluminescence [13,14], have been integrated with PADs for field detection, which greatly broaden their application prospects. Among them, colorimetric PADs are particularly advantageous due to their simplicity and visual readout, especially when combined with smartphones for real-time and quantitative analysis. Nevertheless, colorimetric PADs still suffer from low sensitivity that hinders their widespread application.

Recently, incorporating nanozymes into colorimetric PADs has aroused enormous concerns since they can significantly amplify the signal output of PADs, thereby improving the sensitivity of PADs. Presently, a variety of nanozymes with peroxidase-like activity have been successfully synthesized and applied in colorimetric PADs for pesticides detection [15–17]. Compared with peroxidase mimetics, oxidase mimetics show prominent advantages since they can directly catalyze the oxidation of peroxidase substrates by oxygen without the involvement of unstable hydrogen oxide, simplifying the detection process and improving accuracy. Despite their unique superiorities, few kinds of oxidase mimetics have been reported that seriously restricts their further applications in colorimetric PADs. Photo-responsive nanozymes bring a close integration of photochemistry and nanoscience, offering a novel avenue for the development of oxidase mimetics. Photo-responsive oxidase mimetics show broad application prospects owing to their environmental friendliness as well as controllable spatial and temporal accuracy [18]. However, up to now, the colorimetric PADs for carbosulfan detection using photo-responsive oxidase mimetics have not been explored. Moreover, it is disappointing that photo-responsive oxidase mimetics are mainly focused on noble metal nanoclusters [19,20], metal/covalent organic frameworks [21,22] and conjugated microporous polymer [23], which are limited to some inherent drawbacks including high cost and complex preparation process. Therefore, developing low-cost and easily prepared photo-responsive nanozymes and investigating their potential in colorimetric PADs for carbosulfan detection is still imperative and challenging.

Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), a metal-free semiconductor, has been widely applied across various domains especially photocatalysis by virtue of its facile synthesis, low cost and high stability. The superior features of g-C3N4 have inspired researchers to explore its photo-responsive oxidase-like activity [24]. However, its photo-responsive oxidase-like activity is greatly hindered by the inefficient utilization of visible light and rapid recombination of photo-generated charge carriers. To address these issues, various strategies including morphology regulation [25], construction of heterojunction [26,27], element doping [28] and modification with carbon materials [29] have been proposed. Among them, modifying g-C3N4 with carbon materials is particularly promising since they can avoid the leaching of metals which is more environmentally friendly. Moreover, it is worth mentioning that they also show the advantages of low cost and high stability. As a novel type of carbon-based materials, carbon quantum dots (C-dots) have been successfully integrated with g-C3N4, which not only broaden the visible light absorption region on the account of its superior up-conversion properties, but also effectively inhibit the recombination of photo-generated charge carriers through the transfer of photo-generated electrons [30].

Inspired by these findings, C-dots/g-C3N4 photo-responsive oxidase mimetic was prepared and employed in the field of colorimetric PAD for carbosulfan detection ( Scheme 1). The oxidase-like activity of g-C3N4 is significantly enhanced after the modification with C-dots that can be ascribed to the enhanced photo-harvesting capability and more efficient charge separation and transfer. Under the illumination of visible light, C-dots/g-C3N4 catalyzes the dissolved oxygen to oxidize colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to form blue oxidized TMB (oxTMB) due to the generation of 1O2 and holes. Specifically, the reductive sulfide compound generated from the hydrolysis of carbosulfan under acidic conditions inhibits the oxidation of TMB. Consequently, a colorimetric assay for carbosulfan detection is constructed. Furthermore, a colorimetric PAD is developed for the detection of carbosulfan with high sensitivity and high accuracy. This work may not only open a new door for the application of photo-responsive nanozymes in colorimetric PADs, but also provides a novel insight into constructing g-C3N4-based photo-responsive nanozymes with high catalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of g-C3N4 and x% C-dots/g-C3N4
ce:section.ce:para: g-C3N4 was synthesized via a thermal polymerization method using urea as the precursor. Initially, 20 g of urea was placed into an alumina crucible. The crucible was then introduced into a muffle furnace, heated at a rate of 0.5 ℃/min to 600 ℃ and maintained for 3 h. After cooling, the resultant solid product was ground into powder, subjected to 5 M HNO3 solution and refluxed at 100 °C for 24 h. After that, the obtained g-C3N4 was thoroughly washed with deionized water until the supernatant was near-neutral pH. The final product was designated as g-C3N4.

The synthesis of x% C-dots/g-C3N4 was conducted using a one-step homogeneous thermal decomposition method [31]. According to this report, the loading of C-dots in the final C-dots/g-C3N4 composites can be readily controlled by the added amount of citric acid. A fixed amount of urea (20 g) and varying amounts of citric acid were utilized as the precursor. Specifically, 10 mg, 40 mg, and 100 mg of citric acid were employed to achieve different doping levels. The heating and acid treatment processes were consistent with the methods for the preparation of g-C3N4. The final solid powders obtained from these steps were designated as 0.05 % C-dots/g-C3N4, 0.2 % C-dots/g-C3N4, and 0.5 % C-dots/g-C3N4, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The photo-responsive oxidase-like activity of C-dots/g-C3N4
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the photo-responsive oxidase-like activity of the synthesized materials, 100 μL of g-C3N4 or x% C-dots/g-C3N4 (0.2 mg/mL) was mixed with 100 μL of TMB (5 mM) in a 800 μL sodium acetate-acetic acid buffer solution (0.2 M, pH 2.8). The mixture was then irradiated at a fixed position under a xenon lamp with a filter above 420 nm for 3 min. Subsequently, the UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using a UV-8000 spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impacts of different scavengers on the photo-responsive oxidase-like activity of C-dots/g-C3N4
ce:section.ce:para: To elucidate the reactive species involved in the catalytic process, various scavengers were introduced into the reaction solutions. A mixed solution composed of 700 μL of sodium acetate-acetic acid buffer solution (pH 2.8), 100 μL of 0.2 % C-dots/g-C3N4 (0.2 mg/mL), 100 μL of TMB (5 mM), and 100 μL of various scavengers was exposed to a xenon lamp irradiation for different durations, after which the UV–vis absorption spectra was recorded. The final concentrations of the various scavengers were as follows: nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NBT, 1 mM), tryptophan (TRP, 0.2 mM), isopropanol (IPA, 1 mM), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na, 0.1 mM), catalase (CAT, 200 U/mL).

Due to the influence of solution pH on the hydrolysis of carbosulfan, the pH value of the buffer solution was firstly optimized before the applications of this assay for carbosulfan detection. Subsequently, 100 μL of 0.2 % C-dots/g-C3N4 (0.2 mg/mL) and 100 μL of TMB (5 mM) were sequentially added to 750 μL of sodium acetate-acetic acid buffer solution (pH 2.8). Finally, 50 μL of carbosulfan solutions of varying concentrations (0–100 μM) were added. And the mixture was exposed to a xenon lamp for 3 minutes. Post-irradiation, the UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the PAD for carbosulfan detection
ce:section.ce:para: For the construction of PAD, filter paper was cut into multiple circular disks with a diameter of 10 mm using a laser cutter. Initially, 20 μL of 0.2 % C-dots/g-C3N4 solution (0.2 mg/mL) was uniformly applied onto the paper disks and subsequently subjected to vacuum drying. Following the drying procedure, the disks were stored in a dark environment for subsequent use. Thereafter, 20 μL of sodium acetate-acetic acid buffer solution (pH 2.8) containing TMB (5 mM) and 20 μL of carbosulfan solutions with varying concentrations (0–100 μM) were uniformly pipetted onto the disks. The disks were then exposed to a xenon lamp for 3 min. In the subsequent steps, the color picker developed by Achim Heynen, which has been widely recognized for its good stability and accuracy in color extraction, was utilized to obtaine the real-time RGB value of the disks for the quantitative analysis of carbosulfan by aligning the disks with the crosshairs of the color picker interface.

Water samples were collected from a lake and soil samples from the lakeside at University of Jinan, as well as apples from a local supermarket. After a series of preparation steps, these samples were processed into usable liquids forms. Subsequently, carbosulfan was introduced into the resulting liquids to prepare solutions with different concentrations. Detection of carbosulfan in the real samples was then carried out using the aforementioned method.

As depicted in Fig. 1A, C-dots/g-C3N4 was synthesized via a simple pyrolysis method. Firstly, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was employed to characterize the morphologies of the as-prepared materials. TEM images of g-C3N4 reveal that it exhibits a layered structure with a two-dimensional nanosheet morphology and occasional interlayer stacking (Fig. 1 and Figure S1). Fig. 1C-E display the TEM images of 0.2 % C-dots/g-C3N4 at various magnifications, revealing an average size of C-dots to be 5.28 ± 0.18 nm with a relatively uniform distribution. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the as-prepared materials are shown in Fig. 1F. A distinct characteristic peak at 27.8° is observed for all samples, corresponding to the (002) interlayer reflection of g-C3N4. This peak noticeably weakens and broadens with the increased doping ratio of C-dots which may be ascribed to the loading of C-dots during the formation of g-C3N4 interacts with the surrounding lattice [31]. Then, Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured to investigate their chemical structure. As shown in Fig. 1G, significant peaks in the range of 1200–1700 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of CN heterocycles. The broad peak at 3250 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching mode of the N-H group [32] and the characteristic peak at 810 cm−1 is associated with the out-of-plane bending vibration of the triazine unit [33]. These features are prominently present in the FTIR spectra of all four materials without any additional peaks, indicating that the basic chemical structure of g-C3N4 was not significantly altered by the doping of C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photo-responsive oxidase-like activity of g-C3N4 and C-dots/g-C3N4
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the photo-responsive oxidase-like activity of g-C3N4 and C-dots/g-C3N4, TMB was selected as the substrate and control experiments were designed. As shown in Fig. 2A, under visible light irradiation, C-dots/g-C3N4 catalyzes the oxidation of TMB resulting in a strong absorption peak centered at 652 nm. In contrast, TMB alone cannot be oxidized under light irradiation, verifying the oxidase-like activity of C-dots/g-C3N4. Additionally, no characteristic absorption peak of oxTMB is observed when mixed C-dots/g-C3N4 with TMB in the absence of light, indicating the necessity of light for its oxidase-like activity. Though g-C3N4 is also able to catalyze TMB oxidation under light irradiation, its absorption peak centered at 652 nm is much lower, which implies that the oxidase-like activity of g-C3N4 is significantly enhanced after modification with C-dots. Then, several reaction conditions including the loading amounts of C-dots, pH of the buffer solution, the concentrations of TMB and C-dots/g-C3N4, irradiation time were optimized to obtain C-dots/g-C3N4 with the maximum oxidase-like activity. Fig. 2B presents a comparative activity analysis among materials with varying C-dots loading amounts, showing that 0.2 % C-dots/g-C3N4 exhibits the highest oxidase-like activity. As shown in Figure S2A and S2B, the optimal pH value is 2.8 and TMB concentration is 0.5 mM. While, the oxidase-like activity of C-dots/g-C3N4 increases gradually with the extension of irradiation time and stays almost unchanged when the irradiation time is longer than 6 min (Figure S2C). Moreover, it gradually increases as the concentration of C-dots/g-C3N4 increases (Figure S2D). Taking high sensitivity, short detection time and low cost into consideration, irradiation time of 3 min and 0.02 mg/mL of 0.2 % C-dots/g-C3N4 were chosen. Under the optimal conditions, its photo-controllable oxidase mimetic activity was further studied. Fig. 2 C shows that the oxidase-like activity of C-dots/g-C3N4 displays a staircase-like behavior by continuously turning the light on and off, allowing precise regulation of catalytic activity and improving detection accuracy. Then, steady-state kinetics and specific activity (SA) were used to compare the oxidase-like activity of 0.2 % C-dots/g-C3N4 and g-C3N4 in depth. It is clearly found that 0.2 % C-dots/g-C3N4 exhibits a lower K m and a higher V max compared with g-C3N4 (Fig. 2D-2E, 2G-2H). Besides, the SA of 0.2 % C-dots/g-C3N4 (0.83 U/mg) is approximately 21 times greater than that of g-C3N4 (0.039 U/mg) (Fig. 2F and 2I). These results undoubtedly prove that the photo-responsive oxidase-like activity of g-C3N4 is dramatically enhanced after modification with C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the photo-responsive oxidase-like activity of C-dots/g-C3N4
ce:section.ce:para: The excellent photo-responsive oxidase-mimicking activity of C-dots/g-C3N4 prompts an exploration of its catalytic mechanism. Previous studies have indicated that photo-harvesting capability is an important factor affecting the catalytic activity of photo-responsive nanozymes [34,35]. Consequently, the ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) of the as-prepared materials were firstly conducted. Fig. 3A shows a significant redshift in the absorption bands towards the visible light region as the loading amounts of C-dots increases, confirming a substantial enhancement in their visible light absorption capacity. Meanwhile, these changes can be also reflected by the progressively deeper color of these materials. The corresponding bandgap (Eg) of each material was determined and shown in Figure S3. With the introduction of C-dots, the Eg decrease gradually from 2.91 eV to 1.66 eV, indicating that the materials become more capable of generating charge carriers and initiating catalytic reactions, which is consistent with previous finding [31]. After the determination of valence band (EVB) of the as-prepared materials (Figure S4), their band structures were established (Fig. 3B). In addition to affecting the electronic structure of g-C3N4, C-dots also influence the photo-generated charge separation and transfer behaviors. Firstly, the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of g-C3N4 and 0.2 % C-dots/g-C3N4 under an excitation wavelength of 380 nm were conducted and the results are presented in Fig. 3C. It is evident the PL intensity significantly decreases after the incorporation of C-dots, reflecting the interaction between C-dots and g-C3N4 that suppresses the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers. Subsequently, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was employed to characterize charge carrier transportation. Fig. 3D shows that the Nyquist semicircle diameter for the 0.2 % C-dots/g-C3N4 curve is notably smaller than that of g-C3N4, indicating lower charge transfer resistance and higher charge carrier separation efficiency for the former. Finally, photo-electrochemical technique was utilized to further investigate charge separation efficiency. Fig. 3E demonstrates that the photocurrent intensity of 0.2 % C-dots/g-C3N4 is significantly higher than that of g-C3N4, which is attributed to the improved transfer and separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers. In summary, the visible light absorption capacity and photo-generated carriers separation efficiency of g-C3N4 are effectively improved after the modification with C-dots. As expected, C-dots/g-C3N4 exhibits superior photo-responsive oxidase-like activity compared to g-C3N4.

It is universally accepted that dissolved O2 is crucial for the catalytic activity of photo-responsive oxidase mimetics. Consequently, the role of O2 is firstly explored by purging N2 or O2 to the reaction solutions before the C-dots/g-C3N4 -catalyzed oxidation reaction of TMB. As shown in Fig. 3F, the absorbance significantly decreases in N2 atmosphere, while it remarkably increases in O2 atmosphere. Hence, O2 plays a vital role in the photo-responsive oxidase-mimicking activity of C-dots/g-C3N4. Moreover, some reactive species including superoxide anion (O2 ·-), singlet oxygen (1O2), hydroxyl radicals (·OH), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and photogenerated holes (h+) may be involved in the catalytic process. Scavengers such as NBT, TRP, IPA, CAT and EDTA-2Na were introduced to the reaction solution to eliminate O2 ·-, 1O2, ·OH, H2O2 and h+, respectively. Preliminary results from Fig. 4A indicate that 1O2 and h+ exhibit a more pronounced inhibitory effect on the catalytic activity of C-dots/g-C3N4, while the impacts of other reactive species are negligible. Subsequently, electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was further employed to explore the formation of 1O2 and h+. The three-line characteristic peaks of TEMP-1O2 adduct with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1 are clearly observed (Fig. 4B). The signal in the dark is probably derived from the energy transfer of molecule oxygen [36]. Meanwhile, the ESR signal of TEMPO-h+ adduct decreases gradually with the prolonged illumination time, confirming the formation of h+ (Fig. 4C). However, no obvious signals are found for both DMPO-·OH adduct (Fig. 4D) and DMPO-O2 ·- adduct (Fig. 4E). As a consequence, 1O2 and h+ are the main reactive species responsible for the oxidase-like activity of C-dots/g-C3N4. Based on these findings, a schematic representation of the possible catalytic mechanism of C-dots/g-C3N4 is depicted in Fig. 4F. Under the illumination of visible light, C-dots/g-C3N4 is excited to generate electrons and holes. The dissolved O2 can be activated to 1O2 by C-dots/g-C3N4. Then, the generated reactive species (1O2, h+) can react with TMB, resulting in its oxidation.

It is imperative to study its application in colorimetric detection of carbosulfan benefiting from the excellent catalytic activity of C-dots/g-C3N4. Previous studies have indicated that carbosulfan is decomposed to reductive sulfide species ([SH]) under acidic conditions due to the cleavage of its internal N-S bond [2,37], which may inhibit the oxidase-like activity of C-dots/g-C3N4. Given the above discussions, a colorimetric platform for carbosulfan detection based on the oxidase-like activity of C-dots/g-C3N4 is established and the detection principle is illustrated in Fig. 5A. To validate the feasibility of this colorimetric assay, different concentrations of carbosulfan were introduced into the detection system, and the corresponding UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded (Fig. 5B). As the concentration of carbosulfan increases, there is a concomitant decrease in the absorbance of the solution. Notably, the proposed detection platform can be used for carbosulfan detection. Considering the importance of solution pH on the hydrolysis of carbosulfan and catalytic activity of C-dots/g-C3N4, the pH of the buffer solution was optimized before its application in carbosulfan detection and the results are depicted in Figure S5. The strongest inhibition was identified when the pH is 2.8. As a result, buffer solution with pH 2.8 was selected for subsequent testing. Then, the C-dots/g-C3N4-TMB system is employed to detect carbosulfan. As illustrated in Fig. 5C, the absorbance at 652 nm continuously decreases as the concentration of carbosulfan increases from 5 to 100 μM. The change in absorbance (ΔA) is linearly related to carbosulfan concentration between 5 and 70 μM (inset of Fig. 5D), with a limit of detection (LOD) of 2.11 μM. The linear equation of ΔA = 0.0103c + 0.0074 (R2 = 0.997) is obtained, where ΔA represents the change in absorbance at 652 nm before and after the introduction of carbosulfan. Subsequently, a detailed study comparing the analytical performance of this method with previously reported methods is conducted and the results are shown in Table S1. It is evident that this method shows a distinguished advantage of short detection time. Additionally, as a carbon-based nanozyme, C-dots/g-C3N4 exhibits environmental friendliness, low production costs, and exceptional stability compared to these metal-based nanozymes, though this method based on C-dots/g-C3N4 shows lower sensitivity than the colorimetric method based on CeO2@carbon nanozymes and Ti3C2/AuPt. Moreover, the chromogenic reaction of C-dots/g-C3N4-catalyzed TMB oxidation in this developed method can be regulated by turning the light on or off, which renders the method with higher accuracy. Although the LC-MS method demonstrates higher sensitivity, it is limited by the long analysis time, substantial use of organic solvent, and costly instrumentation. In summary, we have developed a time-saving, low-cost, environment friendly and highly accurate method for carbosulfan detection based on C-dots/g-C3N4 nanozyme.

Furthermore, the selectivity and anti-interference ability of this detection system were assessed by individually adding other pesticides including neonicotine pesticides (thiamethoxam, acetamiprid, imidacloprid and clothianidin), organochlorine pesticides (methylviologen), carbamate pesticides (carbaryl) and organophosphate pesticides (glyphosate) or simultaneously introducing them with carbosulfan. Fig. 5E shows that the absorbance is basically the same as that of the blank solution when these pesticides were added alone, whereas it is significantly reduced after the introduction of carbosulfan, suggesting the high selectivity of this sensing platform. Additionally, the absorbance of this detection system stays almost unchanged when introducing carbosulfan coexisting with other pesticides, which indicates the good anti-interference ability of this detection system.

In the light of remarkable oxidase-like activity of C-dots/g-C3N4, high efficiency and low cost of on-site analysis, a colorimetric PAD for carbosulfan detection using a smartphone has been developed. As depicted in Fig. 6A, C-dots/g-C3N4 catalyzes the oxidation of colorless TMB to form oxTMB accompanied by the generation of blue color on the PAD. However, the presence of carbosulfan inhibits the chromogenic reaction, resulting in the color on the PADs changing from dark blue to nearly colorless with the increasing concentrations of carbosulfan. The R/B ratio of the PADs increases gradually with the increasing concentrations of carbosulfan (Fig. 6B). A good linear relationship between R/B ratio and carbosulfan concentrations within the range of 5–60 μM is found (Fig. 6C). The linear regression equation is Y = 0.0048 X + 0.6291 (R2 = 0.992), with an LOD of 2.52 μM, where Y represents the R/B value and X denotes the carbosulfan concentration.

Given carbosulfan is commonly used in agricultural production to control pests, its residues are likely to remain in water sources, soil and fruits. Therefore, spiked recovery experiments were conducted using samples from lake water, soil and apple to evaluate the practical applicability of the developed sensing platform. As shown in Table 1, recoveries by colorimetric method are in the range of 96.0 %-109.5 % with relative standard deviations (RSD) below 5.2 %. Moreover, recoveries by PAD are in the range of 90.0 %-110.0 % with RSD less than 9.5 % (Table S2-S4). These results sufficiently demonstrate the feasibility and reliability of the proposed detection system in practical applications.

In summary, a colorimetric assay for carbosulfan detection base on the C-dots/g-C3N4 photo-responsive nanozyme is reported for the first time. C-dots/g-C3N4 photo-responsive nanozyme with environmental favorability, simple preparation process and low production cost has been successfully prepared through a one-step homogeneous thermal decomposition method and its catalytic mechanism is studied in detail. On the basis that reductive sulfide compound obtained from the hydrolysis of carbosulfan under acidic medium inhibits TMB oxidation catalyzed by C-dots/g-C3N4, a colorimetric method is proposed and a corresponding PAD is established. The detection limits for colorimetric mode and PAD are 2.11 and 2.52 μM, respectively. Moreover, satisfactory results are obtained when both colorimetric method and PAD are applied in real samples detection. The developed method showcases the benefits of a rapid detection time, cost-effectiveness, environmental friendliness and high precision. This research not only pioneers an innovative strategy for the fabrication of photo-responsive nanozymes with extraordinary oxidase-like activity, but also broadens the scope of their application in colorimetric PADs for the detection of carbosulfan.

Haodong Yang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Mingyuan Xia: Validation, Conceptualization. Wanqi Yang: Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis. Hanmeng Liu: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Pengjuan Ni: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Yizhong Lu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32202145 and 22172063), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2022QB192), the Independent Cultivation Program of Innovation Team of Jinan City (2021GXRC052), the Key Project of Anhui Provincial Department of Education (2023AH050869).

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Haodong Yang received his bachelor's degree from University of Jinan in 2023. Currently, he is pursuing his master's degree under the supervision of Dr. Pengjuan Ni. His scientific interests are focused on carbon nitride-based photo-responsive nanozymes.

Mingyuan Xia received his master's degree from University of Jinan in 2023. Currently, he is pursuing his Ph.D. under the supervision of Dr. Yizhong Lu. His scientific interests are focused on nanozymes and colorimetric assays.

Wanqi Yang received her bachelor's degree from Ludong University in 2022. Currently, she is pursuing his master's degree under the supervision of Dr. Pengjuan Ni. Her scientific interests are focused on carbon dots-based photo-responsive nanozymes.

Dr. Hanmeng Liu received her Ph.D. from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2021. Currently, she works in the School of Pharmacy, Anhui University of Chinese Medicine. Her scientific interests are focused on electrochemisty and nanobiosensing.

Dr. Pengjuan Ni received her bachelor’s degree from Hebei University in 2011. She obtained the Ph.D. from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2017. Currently, she works as an associate professor in the School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan. Her scientific interests are focused on photo-responsive nanozymes and colorimetric/fluorescent sensors.

Dr. Yizhong Lu received his Ph.D. from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, in 2015. He then worked as a research fellow at Nanyang Technological University & Cambridge Center for Advanced Research in Energy Efficiency in Singapore. Currently, he is a full proferssor in the School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Shandong. His research interests are focused on size-controlled synthesis, characterization, and size-dependent property study of metal nanocusters, nanobiosensing, and electrochemisty.
